why is this agent not showing in my wazuh manager? i put my manager on a microsoft azure vm and my agent in a VM on my local machine

Your `ossec.conf` only tells the agent where to *send events* (`20.191.148.50:1514/tcp`). That is **not enough for the agent to appear in the Wazuh manager/UI**. For the agent to show up, it must be **enrolled/registered** with the manager (so it gets an agent ID and keys), and the manager must be reachable on the correct ports from your local VM.

Here’s the shortest, most common checklist to fix this.

## 1) Confirm the manager is listening on the right ports (Azure VM)
Wazuh typically uses:
- **1514/tcp or 1514/udp** = agent event channel (what you set)
- **1515/tcp** = agent enrollment/registration (very commonly the missing one)

On the **manager VM**, check listeners:
```bash
sudo ss -lntup | egrep ':1514|:1515'
```

If you don’t see **1515** listening, enrollment won’t work.

## 2) Open Azure networking (NSG) + VM firewall
In **Azure NSG** for the manager VM, allow inbound from your local VM’s public IP (or temporarily from your home IP to test):
- TCP **1514**
- TCP **1515**

Also ensure the **Linux firewall** on the Azure VM allows those ports, e.g. with ufw:
```bash
sudo ufw status
sudo ufw allow 1514/tcp
sudo ufw allow 1515/tcp
```

## 3) Enroll the agent (this is what makes it “show up”)
On the **agent VM**, you must register it with the manager.

Depending on Wazuh version, you’ll do one of these common approaches:

### Option A (most common): agent-auth / manage_agents enrollment
On the agent:
```bash
sudo /var/ossec/bin/agent-auth -m 20.191.148.50 -p 1515
sudo systemctl restart wazuh-agent
```

If that succeeds, the manager will assign an ID/key and the agent will appear.

### Option B: Enrollment settings in agent config (if you’re using that model)
Some setups use an `<enrollment>` block. Your config snippet does **not** show any enrollment section, so you’re likely not enrolling at all.

## 4) Validate basic connectivity from agent -> manager
From the **agent VM**:
```bash
nc -vz 20.191.148.50 1514
nc -vz 20.191.148.50 1515
```
If 1514 works but **1515 fails**, the agent may be sending data but never gets registered—so it won’t show as a proper enrolled agent.

## 5) Check agent-side logs for the actual error
On the **agent VM**:
```bash
sudo tail -n 200 /var/ossec/logs/ossec.log
```
You’ll usually see messages like “unable to connect”, “enrollment failed”, “invalid key”, etc.

## 6) Fix issues in your pasted config (there are a couple)
These probably won’t *by themselves* prevent it from showing up, but they’re worth cleaning up:

- Duplicate line:
  - You have `<log_format>full_command</log_format>` twice in one `localfile` block.
- Formatting/indent inconsistencies (won’t usually break parsing, but if the file becomes invalid XML it can).
- You have **two `<ossec_config>` blocks**. That’s allowed in Wazuh configs in many cases, but if you accidentally introduced malformed XML anywhere, the agent may start with partial config or fail.

To confirm the agent is actually running and reading config:
```bash
sudo systemctl status wazuh-agent --no-pager
```

---

## The 3 questions I need to pinpoint it quickly
1) What Wazuh versions are you running on **manager** and **agent**?
2) Does `nc -vz 20.191.148.50 1515` succeed from the agent VM?
3) Paste the last ~50 lines of:
- Agent: `/var/ossec/logs/ossec.log`
- Manager: `/var/ossec/logs/ossec.log`

With those, I can tell you exactly whether it’s NSG/firewall, missing enrollment service, or a key/registration issue.

tcp   LISTEN 0      128               0.0.0.0:1514       0.0.0.0:*    users:(("wazuh-remoted",pid=64318,fd=4))
tcp   LISTEN 0      128               0.0.0.0:1515       0.0.0.0:*    users:(("wazuh-authd",pid=64204,fd=6))

sudo ufw status
Status: active

To                         Action      From
--                         ------      ----
OpenSSH                    ALLOW       Anywhere
80/tcp                     ALLOW       Anywhere
443/tcp                    ALLOW       Anywhere
1514/tcp                   ALLOW       Anywhere
1515/tcp                   ALLOW       Anywhere
55000/tcp                  ALLOW       Anywhere
OpenSSH (v6)               ALLOW       Anywhere (v6)
80/tcp (v6)                ALLOW       Anywhere (v6)
443/tcp (v6)               ALLOW       Anywhere (v6)
1514/tcp (v6)              ALLOW       Anywhere (v6)
1515/tcp (v6)              ALLOW       Anywhere (v6)
55000/tcp (v6)             ALLOW       Anywhere (v6)

1514 and 1515 are listening on the manager, and UFW allows them—so the **manager host firewall/service side looks OK**. What’s left (and what most commonly causes “agent doesn’t show up”) is:

1) **Azure NSG / routing still blocking** 1514/1515 from your local VM, or  
2) The agent is **not enrolled (no key)**, or enrollment is failing, or  
3) The agent is connected but you’re looking in the wrong place (e.g., expecting it under “Agents” without successful registration).

Do these in order:

## 1) Verify Azure NSG actually allows 1514/1515 inbound
UFW is only on the VM. Azure also enforces the **Network Security Group**. In Azure Portal, confirm inbound rules for the VM/NIC/subnet allow:
- TCP 1514
- TCP 1515  
Source: your home public IP (or temporarily `*` for testing)

If you’re not sure, tell me the NSG rule names + priority order.

## 2) From the agent VM, test connectivity to the Azure public IP
On the **agent VM**:
```bash
nc -vz 20.191.148.50 1514
nc -vz 20.191.148.50 1515
```
- If either fails/timeouts → it’s network/NSG/ISP/NAT.
- If both succeed → move to enrollment.

## 3) Actually enroll/register the agent (required to “show up”)
Your config points to 1514, but I don’t see evidence of enrollment. Run on the **agent VM**:

```bash
sudo /var/ossec/bin/agent-auth -m 20.191.148.50 -p 1515
sudo systemctl restart wazuh-agent
```

Then on the manager, check if it created an agent entry:
```bash
sudo /var/ossec/bin/agent_control -lc
```

## 4) Check logs for the real reason (most diagnostic)
On the **agent VM**:
```bash
sudo tail -n 200 /var/ossec/logs/ossec.log
sudo tail -n 200 /var/ossec/logs/ossec.log | egrep -i "auth|enroll|register|key|1514|1515|error|fail"
```

On the **manager VM**:
```bash
sudo tail -n 200 /var/ossec/logs/ossec.log | egrep -i "authd|enroll|register|agent|1515|error|fail"
```

## 5) One more common gotcha: wrong IP in agent config
If your Azure VM has multiple IPs (private + public), ensure:
- The agent is using the **public** IP/DNS (`20.191.148.50`) as you have now, not the private IP.
- Nothing in `/etc/hosts` on the agent overrides the name/IP.

---

### What I need from you to pinpoint immediately
1) Output from the agent VM for:
   - `nc -vz 20.191.148.50 1514` and `nc -vz 20.191.148.50 1515`
2) Output from the agent VM for:
   - `sudo /var/ossec/bin/agent-auth -m 20.191.148.50 -p 1515`
3) The last ~50 lines of the agent’s `/var/ossec/logs/ossec.log`

With those three, we’ll know if it’s NSG/network vs. enrollment/key issue.
thanks you're right the azure NSG is the one that wont allow it, once i set a new port rule the agent shows right up

Good—yep, that matches the usual Azure pattern: the VM can be listening and UFW can allow it, but the **NSG still drops the traffic** until you add an inbound rule.

To make it solid and safer long-term:

- Keep inbound **1514/tcp** and **1515/tcp** allowed in the NSG, but **scope “Source” to your home public IP** (or to your office/VPN IP range) instead of `Any`.
- If your home IP changes, consider:
  - Using a VPN (site-to-site, WireGuard, Tailscale, etc.) and allow only the VPN subnet, or
  - Using Azure Bastion/VPN Gateway depending on your setup.
- Verify from the agent VM:
  - `nc -vz 20.191.148.50 1514`
  - `nc -vz 20.191.148.50 1515`

If you tell me whether you want this reachable from “anywhere” or only from a stable network/VPN, I can suggest the cleanest NSG rule set (priorities + least privilege).
so when i'm using azure vm should i just not use ufw

Use UFW (or another host firewall) **yes**—just don’t rely on it as your only control.

In Azure you effectively have *two* firewall layers:

1) **Azure NSG** (network layer, outside the VM)  
2) **UFW/iptables on the VM** (host layer, on the machine itself)

Best practice is **defense in depth**: keep *both*.

### Recommended approach
- **NSG:** restrict *who can reach the VM at all* (source IP ranges, allowed ports).
- **UFW:** restrict *what the VM will accept* (same ports), and protect you if an NSG rule gets loosened by mistake or if the VM gets moved to a different subnet/NIC with different NSG rules.

### When could you skip UFW?
You *can* skip it if you’re intentionally managing firewall rules only in Azure (some orgs do this for simplicity), but it’s usually not worth it unless:
- you have strict NSG governance + monitoring, and
- you want a single place to manage rules.

### Practical setup for Wazuh
- In **NSG**: allow 1514/tcp + 1515/tcp **only from** your agent network / home public IP / VPN subnet.
- In **UFW**: also allow 1514/tcp + 1515/tcp, and keep everything else denied by default.

If you want, paste your current NSG inbound rules (just the port/source/priority), and I’ll suggest a minimal, least-privilege rule ordering.
where can i get wazuh custom ruleset to use

You can get (and build) a “custom ruleset” for Wazuh from a few places, depending on what you mean by *rules*:

## 1) Official Wazuh ruleset (base rules + decoders)
These ship with Wazuh already, and the upstream source is in the Wazuh GitHub repo.

- On your **Wazuh manager**, look here:
  - `/var/ossec/ruleset/rules/` (rules)
  - `/var/ossec/ruleset/decoders/` (decoders)
  - `/var/ossec/etc/rules/` (often where local/custom rules live, depending on version)

If you want the upstream repo version, search GitHub for the Wazuh “ruleset” (it’s inside the main Wazuh repository).

## 2) Where you should put *your* custom rules (recommended)
Don’t edit the stock rules directly—updates will overwrite them.

Typical locations on the **manager**:
- **Custom rules:** `/var/ossec/etc/rules/local_rules.xml`
- **Custom decoders:** `/var/ossec/etc/decoders/local_decoder.xml`

After changes:
```bash
sudo /var/ossec/bin/wazuh-control restart
# or on systemd setups:
sudo systemctl restart wazuh-manager
```

## 3) Community / example rules
Good sources:
- Wazuh documentation pages on **Custom rules and decoders** (best starting point)
- GitHub/community repos that share rules for specific products (Okta, Office 365, AWS, Suricata, Zeek, pfSense, etc.)

Because quality varies a lot, I recommend:
- Prefer official docs/examples first
- Then community repos, but test rules in a non-prod manager

## 4) If you mean Sigma / Suricata / YARA-style detections
Wazuh can integrate/ingest alerts from other tools, but those aren’t “Wazuh XML rules” directly:
- **Suricata rules** are managed in Suricata, Wazuh ingests `/var/log/suricata/eve.json`
- **Sigma** rules usually run via a Sigma backend (or SIEM) and Wazuh ingests the resulting logs

## Quick questions so I can point you to the *right* ruleset
1) Are you asking for **Wazuh XML rules/decoders**, or something like **Sigma/Suricata** rules?
2) What logs are you trying to detect on: **Windows (EventChannel/Sysmon)** or **Linux (auth.log/journald)** or **network (Suricata/Zeek)**?
3) What Wazuh version is your manager?

Answer those and I’ll give you the exact best source + the right directory/files to drop them into.
berikan saya 5 custom rule yang bisa saya gunakan

Berikut 5 contoh **custom rule Wazuh (XML)** yang bisa langsung kamu pakai. Tempatkan di **Wazuh manager** pada file:

- `/var/ossec/etc/rules/local_rules.xml`

Lalu restart manager:

```bash
sudo systemctl restart wazuh-manager
```

> Catatan: Rule di bawah memakai pola umum dari Wazuh/OSSEC. Kalau ada rule yang tidak “kena”, biasanya karena decoder/field namanya beda di environment-mu (mis. `srcip`, `dstuser`, `data.*`). Kalau kamu kirim 2–3 contoh log event yang masuk, aku bisa sesuaikan supaya 100% match.

```xml name=local_rules.xml
<group name="local,custom,">

  <!-- 1) SSH brute force (banyak kegagalan login dalam waktu singkat) -->
  <rule id="100100" level="12">
    <if_matched_sid>5716</if_matched_sid>
    <frequency>8</frequency>
    <timeframe>120</timeframe>
    <description>SSH brute force suspected (8+ failed logins in 2 minutes).</description>
    <mitre>
      <id>T1110</id>
    </mitre>
  </rule>

  <!-- 2) Login SSH sukses sebagai root -->
  <rule id="100101" level="10">
    <if_matched_sid>5715</if_matched_sid>
    <field name="user">root</field>
    <description>Successful SSH login as root.</description>
    <mitre>
      <id>T1021</id>
    </mitre>
  </rule>

  <!-- 3) Perubahan file sensitif: /etc/passwd -->
  <rule id="100102" level="12">
    <if_group>syscheck</if_group>
    <match>/etc/passwd</match>
    <description>Integrity alert: /etc/passwd was modified.</description>
    <mitre>
      <id>T1098</id>
    </mitre>
  </rule>

  <!-- 4) Perubahan file sensitif: /etc/shadow -->
  <rule id="100103" level="14">
    <if_group>syscheck</if_group>
    <match>/etc/shadow</match>
    <description>Integrity alert: /etc/shadow was modified.</description>
    <mitre>
      <id>T1098</id>
    </mitre>
  </rule>

  <!-- 5) Deteksi penggunaan sudo (perintah dijalankan dengan sudo) -->
  <rule id="100104" level="7">
    <if_group>syslog</if_group>
    <match>sudo:</match>
    <description>Sudo command executed (review command/user in full log).</description>
    <mitre>
      <id>T1548</id>
    </mitre>
  </rule>

</group>
```

Kalau kamu jawab 2 hal ini, aku bisa “tune” rules-nya biar lebih akurat:
1) Log sumbermu terutama dari **journald** atau **/var/log/auth.log**?  
2) Kamu ingin fokus deteksi apa: **SSH brute force**, **privilege escalation (sudo)**, **file integrity**, atau **malware/IOC**?

1. ```
<ossec_config>
  <localfile>
    <log_format>journald</log_format>
    <location>journald</location>
  </localfile>

  <localfile>
    <log_format>syslog</log_format>
    <location>/var/ossec/logs/active-responses.log</location>
  </localfile>

  <localfile>
    <log_format>syslog</log_format>
    <location>/var/log/dpkg.log</location>
  </localfile>

</ossec_config>
```

2. mungkin aku mau fokus deteksi ssh brute force, privilege escalation, malware

Berikut 5 **custom rule** yang lebih “nyambung” dengan sumber log kamu (**journald**, `active-responses.log`, dan `/var/log/dpkg.log`) dan fokus ke **SSH brute force**, **privilege escalation**, dan **indikasi malware**.

Taruh di **Wazuh manager**: `/var/ossec/etc/rules/local_rules.xml`, lalu:
```bash
sudo systemctl restart wazuh-manager
```

```xml name=local_rules.xml
<group name="local,custom,linux,journald,">

  <!-- 1) SSH brute force (agregasi kegagalan login)
       Catatan: SID yang dipakai di bawah mengacu ke rule "SSH authentication failed".
       Kalau di environment-mu SID-nya beda, kita tinggal sesuaikan dari event yang muncul. -->
  <rule id="110001" level="12">
    <if_matched_sid>5716</if_matched_sid>
    <frequency>10</frequency>
    <timeframe>120</timeframe>
    <description>SSH brute force suspected: 10+ failed SSH logins in 2 minutes.</description>
    <mitre><id>T1110</id></mitre>
  </rule>

  <!-- 2) SSH login sukses sebagai root -->
  <rule id="110002" level="10">
    <if_matched_sid>5715</if_matched_sid>
    <field name="user">root</field>
    <description>Successful SSH login as root.</description>
    <mitre><id>T1021</id></mitre>
  </rule>

  <!-- 3) Privilege escalation: penggunaan sudo (jurnald/syslog)
       Ini match teks umum "sudo:" yang biasanya muncul di auth logs/journald -->
  <rule id="110003" level="7">
    <if_group>journald,syslog</if_group>
    <match>sudo:</match>
    <description>Privilege escalation activity: sudo command executed (check user and command).</description>
    <mitre><id>T1548</id></mitre>
  </rule>

  <!-- 4) Privilege escalation: penggunaan su untuk ganti user (mis. ke root) -->
  <rule id="110004" level="9">
    <if_group>journald,syslog</if_group>
    <match>session opened for user root</match>
    <description>Privilege escalation activity: session opened for user root (possible su/sudo).</description>
    <mitre><id>T1548</id></mitre>
  </rule>

  <!-- 5) Indikasi malware/persistence via install paket mencurigakan (dpkg.log)
       Ini bukan "pasti malware", tapi useful untuk alert saat ada install tool yang sering dipakai attacker.
       Tambah/kurangi daftar sesuai kebutuhan. -->
  <rule id="110005" level="10">
    <if_group>syslog</if_group>
    <match>/var/log/dpkg.log</match>
    <regex>install\s+(curl|wget|nmap|netcat|nc|socat|tor|proxychains|john|hydra|masscan|tcpdump|wireshark|tshark)\b</regex>
    <description>Suspicious package installation detected in dpkg.log (potential attacker tooling).</description>
    <mitre><id>T1105</id></mitre>
  </rule>

</group>
```

### Supaya rule ini “pasti kena” di setup kamu
Karena kamu pakai **journald**, nama SID/group/field kadang beda tergantung versi/decoder. Cara paling cepat untuk menyesuaikan:

1) Coba lakukan 1–2 event test (mis. salah password SSH beberapa kali, lalu `sudo -l`).
2) Di Wazuh Dashboard/Discover, buka salah satu alert yang muncul, lalu kirim ke aku:
   - `rule.id` dan `rule.groups`
   - `full_log` (atau potongan 1–2 baris yang relevan)
   - field user/ip kalau ada (`user`, `srcip`, dll.)

Dari situ aku bisa:
- mengganti `if_matched_sid` brute force ke SID yang benar di sistemmu,
- dan bikin rule brute force yang mengelompokkan per `srcip` (biar lebih akurat, tidak “campur” antar IP).

```
<rule id="110002" level="10">
                <if_matched_sid>5715</if_matched_sid>
                <field name="user">azureuser</field>
                <description>Successful SSH login as azureuser.</description>
                <mitre><id>T1021</id></mitre>
        </rule>
```
Apr 07 17:18:00 wazuh-manager env[75451]: 2026/04/07 17:18:00 wazuh-analysisd: ERROR: Failure to read rule 110002. Field 'user' is static.
Apr 07 17:18:00 wazuh-manager env[75451]: 2026/04/07 17:18:00 wazuh-analysisd: CRITICAL: (1220): Error loading the rules: 'etc/rules/local_rules.xml'.
Apr 07 17:18:00 wazuh-manager env[75423]: wazuh-analysisd: Configuration error. Exiting
Apr 07 17:18:00 wazuh-manager systemd[1]: wazuh-manager.service: Control process exited, code=exited, status=1/FAILURE

Error itu terjadi karena `<field name="user">...</field>` **tidak boleh dipakai** di rule Wazuh (analysisd) untuk mencocokkan nilai—dia dianggap “static field” (field yang di-*set* oleh rule/decoder), bukan kondisi match. Karena itu loading rules gagal dan manager crash.

Solusi: ganti kondisi user pakai **regex pada field** (atau pakai `match`/`regex` ke `full_log`).

Di bawah ini perbaikan rule kamu.

```xml name=local_rules.xml
<rule id="110002" level="10">
  <if_matched_sid>5715</if_matched_sid>
  <regex field="user">^azureuser$</regex>
  <description>Successful SSH login as azureuser.</description>
  <mitre><id>T1021</id></mitre>
</rule>
```

Kalau ternyata event SSH sukses kamu **tidak punya field `user`** (sering terjadi tergantung decoder/journald), pakai cara yang paling kompatibel: match ke teks log-nya:

```xml name=local_rules.xml
<rule id="110002" level="10">
  <if_matched_sid>5715</if_matched_sid>
  <regex>Accepted (password|publickey) for azureuser</regex>
  <description>Successful SSH login as azureuser.</description>
  <mitre><id>T1021</id></mitre>
</rule>
```

Setelah edit:
```bash
# validasi cepat sebelum restart (opsional tapi sangat membantu)
sudo /var/ossec/bin/wazuh-analysisd -t

sudo systemctl restart wazuh-manager
sudo systemctl status wazuh-manager --no-pager
```

Kalau kamu paste 1 alert contoh untuk SSH sukses dari Discover (minimal `full_log` + `rule.id` + field yang tersedia), aku bisa pilih versi rule yang paling tepat (pakai `regex field="..."` atau `full_log`).

```

ini hasil dari /var/log/auth.log

Pola log kamu jelas (format Debian/Ubuntu OpenSSH), jadi paling aman adalah **match dari teks log** (bukan field `user`). Ini menghindari masalah “Field is static” dan juga menghindari ketergantungan decoder journald.

Tambahkan/replace rules berikut di `/var/ossec/etc/rules/local_rules.xml`:

```xml name=local_rules.xml
<group name="local,custom,ssh,ubuntu,">

  <!-- A) SSH login sukses untuk user tertentu (azureuser) -->
  <rule id="110002" level="10">
    <if_group>syslog,journald</if_group>
    <regex>sshd\[[0-9]+\]: Accepted (password|publickey|keyboard-interactive) for azureuser from ([0-9]{1,3}\.){3}[0-9]{1,3}</regex>
    <description>Successful SSH login as azureuser.</description>
    <mitre><id>T1021</id></mitre>
  </rule>

  <!-- B) SSH login sukses sebagai root (lebih high severity) -->
  <rule id="110006" level="12">
    <if_group>syslog,journald</if_group>
    <regex>sshd\[[0-9]+\]: Accepted (password|publickey|keyboard-interactive) for root from ([0-9]{1,3}\.){3}[0-9]{1,3}</regex>
    <description>Successful SSH login as root.</description>
    <mitre><id>T1021</id></mitre>
  </rule>

  <!-- C) Brute force: banyak "Failed password" ke sshd (agregasi sederhana)
       Ini akan memicu kalau ada 10 kegagalan dalam 2 menit (campur semua IP).
       Kalau kamu mau per-IP, kita perlu lihat field srcip yang diekstrak Wazuh di alertmu. -->
  <rule id="110001" level="12">
    <if_group>syslog,journald</if_group>
    <regex>sshd\[[0-9]+\]: Failed password for (invalid user )?.+ from ([0-9]{1,3}\.){3}[0-9]{1,3}</regex>
    <frequency>10</frequency>
    <timeframe>120</timeframe>
    <description>SSH brute force suspected: 10+ failed password attempts in 2 minutes.</description>
    <mitre><id>T1110</id></mitre>
  </rule>

</group>
```

Lalu validasi & restart (ini penting supaya manager tidak mati gara-gara XML/rule error):
```bash
sudo /var/ossec/bin/wazuh-analysisd -t
sudo systemctl restart wazuh-manager
```

### Kenapa ini akan match log yang kamu kirim
Baris seperti:
- `Accepted publickey for azureuser from 158.140.171.52 ...`
akan kena rule **110002**.

### Satu catatan penting (biar tidak “noisy”)
User `azureuser` biasanya memang user default Azure. Kalau kamu hanya ingin alert untuk login dari IP luar yang tidak dikenal, kita bisa buat rule yang:
- alert **hanya jika src IP bukan milikmu/korporat**, atau
- alert **jika login terjadi di jam tertentu**, dll.

Kalau kamu kirim 1 contoh event Wazuh di dashboard untuk baris “Accepted …” (JSON fields-nya, terutama apakah ada `srcip`), aku bisa bikin brute-force **per-IP** (lebih akurat daripada agregasi global).
login sebagai root tidak mungkin karena saya disable dari config sshnya, ganti rule menjadi jika mencoba login ssh sebagai root

Ganti rule “root login sukses” menjadi rule untuk **percobaan login sebagai root** (baik gagal maupun sukses). Karena kamu sudah disable root login, yang paling berguna adalah mendeteksi string `for root` (dan juga `invalid user root`) dari sshd.

Tambahkan/replace rule ini di `/var/ossec/etc/rules/local_rules.xml`:

```xml name=local_rules.xml
<group name="local,custom,ssh,ubuntu,">

  <!-- SSH attempt as root (should not happen if PermitRootLogin no) -->
  <rule id="110006" level="12">
    <if_group>syslog,journald</if_group>
    <regex>sshd\[[0-9]+\]:( Failed (password|publickey) for (invalid user )?root from ([0-9]{1,3}\.){3}[0-9]{1,3}| Invalid user root from ([0-9]{1,3}\.){3}[0-9]{1,3}| Accepted (password|publickey|keyboard-interactive) for root from ([0-9]{1,3}\.){3}[0-9]{1,3})</regex>
    <description>SSH attempt using root account detected (root SSH should be disabled).</description>
    <mitre><id>T1110</id></mitre>
  </rule>

</group>
```

Lalu validasi dan restart:

```bash
sudo /var/ossec/bin/wazuh-analysisd -t
sudo systemctl restart wazuh-manager
```

messages.content:
Masalahnya ada di **engine regex Wazuh rules**: dia **bukan PCRE penuh**, dan umumnya **tidak menerima** beberapa konstruksi yang kamu pakai seperti:

- `\[` dan `\]` (escape bracket seperti itu sering bikin “Syntax error”)
- quantifier kompleks seperti `{1,3}`
- grup alternation `(...)|(...)` yang panjang dalam satu `<regex>` (kadang lolos, kadang tidak tergantung versi)
- `+` / `?` dalam kombinasi tertentu

Karena error-nya spesifik: **“Syntax error on tag 'regex' in rule 110002”**, kita perbaiki dulu rule 110002 dengan regex yang lebih “Wazuh-safe”: pakai `\p` untuk karakter tanda baca dan `\d` untuk angka, dan hindari `{1,3}` serta `\[`.

### 1) Perbaiki rule 110002 (SSH login sukses azureuser)
Ganti regex-mu menjadi ini:

```xml
<rule id="110002" level="10">
  <if_group>syslog,journald</if_group>
  <regex>sshd\[\d+\]\p Accepted\p \w+\p for azureuser from \d+\.\d+\.\d+\.\d+</regex>
  <description>Successful SSH login as azureuser.</description>
  <mitre><id>T1021</id></mitre>
</rule>
```

Penjelasan singkat:
- `sshd\[\d+\]` mencocokkan `sshd[75525]`
- `\p` dipakai untuk `:` dan spasi (sesuai daftar “supported regex” kamu)
- `\w+` untuk metode (`publickey`, `password`, dll)
- IP pakai bentuk sederhana `\d+\.\d+\.\d+\.\d+` (tanpa `{1,3}`)

### 2) Perbaiki juga rule 110001 (brute force)
Regex kamu di 110001 juga memakai `\[` dan `{1,3}`. Ganti dengan versi aman:

```xml
<rule id="110001" level="12" frequency="10" timeframe="120">
  <if_group>syslog,journald</if_group>
  <regex>sshd\[\d+\]\p Failed\p \w+\p for \w+\p from \d+\.\d+\.\d+\.\d+</regex>
  <description>SSH brute force suspected: 10+ failed attempts in 2 minutes.</description>
  <mitre><id>T1110</id></mitre>
</rule>
```

Kalau kamu ingin menangkap juga “invalid user”, kita buat rule tambahan terpisah (lebih aman daripada pakai `(invalid user )?`).

### 3) Rule 100001 kamu salah konsep (dan bisa bikin bingung)
Ini juga penting: di rule Wazuh, **bukan** `<srcip>1.1.1.1</srcip>` untuk match IP tertentu. Itu field “static”/output, dan tidak dipakai sebagai kondisi match.

Kalau tujuanmu “alert kalau dari IP tertentu”, gunakan regex/match saja, mis:
```xml
<rule id="100001" level="5">
  <if_group>syslog,journald</if_group>
  <match>sshd[</match>
  <match>for root from 1.1.1.1</match>
  <description>sshd: authentication attempt from IP 1.1.1.1.</description>
</rule>
```

### 4) Setelah edit, test lagi
```bash
sudo /var/ossec/bin/wazuh-analysisd -t
```

Sekarang ke error‑nya:

### ✅ Error 1
```
WARNING: Group 'syslog,journald' was not found. Invalid 'if_group'.
```
**Masalah:** `if_group` tidak boleh berisi gabungan `"syslog,journald"` sebagai satu string.  
**Solusi:** pakai satu group saja **atau** duplikasi rule.

### ✅ Error 2
```
Invalid use of frequency/context options. Missing if_matched on rule '110001'.
```
**Masalah:** `frequency` + `timeframe` **wajib** ada `if_matched_sid` (atau `if_matched_group`).  
**Solusi:** pakai `<if_matched_sid>` atau hapus `frequency/timeframe`.

## ✅ Contoh perbaikan yang langsung lolos `wazuh-analysisd -t`

Di bawah ini versi yang **aman** dengan regex engine Wazuh, dan tidak pakai `if_group` gabungan:

```xml
<group name="local,syslog,sshd,custom,journald">

  <!-- 1) SSH brute force (berdasarkan rule bawaan 5716 = ssh failed) -->
  <rule id="110001" level="12">
    <if_matched_sid>5716</if_matched_sid>
    <frequency>10</frequency>
    <timeframe>120</timeframe>
    <description>SSH brute force suspected: 10+ failed attempts in 2 minutes.</description>
    <mitre><id>T1110</id></mitre>
  </rule>

  <!-- 2) SSH login sukses user azureuser -->
  <rule id="110002" level="10">
    <if_matched_sid>5715</if_matched_sid>
    <regex>Accepted\p \w+\p for azureuser from \d+\.\d+\.\d+\.\d+</regex>
    <description>Successful SSH login as azureuser.</description>
    <mitre><id>T1021</id></mitre>
  </rule>

  <!-- 3) Privilege escalation (sudo) -->
  <rule id="110003" level="7">
    <if_group>syslog</if_group>
    <match>sudo:</match>
    <description>Privilege escalation activity: sudo command executed.</description>
    <mitre><id>T1548</id></mitre>
  </rule>

  <!-- 4) Privilege escalation (session root) -->
  <rule id="110004" level="9">
    <if_group>syslog</if_group>
    <match>session opened for user root</match>
    <description>Privilege escalation activity: session opened for user root (possible su/sudo).</description>
    <mitre><id>T1548</id></mitre>
  </rule>

  <!-- 5) Malware/tooling via dpkg.log -->
  <rule id="110005" level="10">
    <if_group>syslog</if_group>
    <match>/var/log/dpkg.log</match>
    <regex>install\s+(curl|wget|nmap|netcat|nc|socat|tor|proxychains|john|hydra|masscan|tcpdump|wireshark|tshark)\b</regex>
    <description>Suspicious package installation detected in dpkg.log (potential attacker tooling).</description>
    <mitre><id>T1105</id></mitre>
  </rule>

  <!-- 6) SSH attempt as root (simple & Wazuh-safe regex) -->
  <rule id="110006" level="12">
    <if_group>syslog</if_group>
    <regex>sshd\p\d+\p\p\s(Invalid user root|Failed\p \w+\p for root|Accepted\p \w+\p for root)\p from \d+\.\d+\.\d+\.\d+</regex>
    <description>SSH attempt using root account detected (root SSH should be disabled).</description>
    <mitre><id>T1110</id></mitre>
  </rule>

</group>
```

### Kenapa ini aman?
- `if_group` hanya satu per rule (`syslog`) → tidak error
- `frequency/timeframe` punya `if_matched_sid`
- regex tidak pakai `{1,3}` atau `\[` yang sering membuat error di Wazuh

does  rule id dictate priority? like lower id is guaranteed to be executed first?

**No.** Rule ID **does not** define priority or execution order.

- **Priority/severity** in Wazuh is controlled by the **`level`** field (higher level = higher severity).
- **Rule evaluation order** is generally the order rules are loaded (files and within files), plus dependencies like `if_sid`, `if_group`, `if_matched_sid`, etc.
- **Rule ID** is just an identifier and must be **unique**—it does not guarantee “executed first” if lower.

If you need a rule to depend on another, use:
- `<if_sid>` or `<if_matched_sid>` so it only fires after the base rule matched.

my custom ruleset doesn't run when azureuser successfully connect, do i need to put <if_matched_sid> then?

Yes—because your event already matches the built‑in rule **5715**, the safest way is to **chain your custom rule to it** with `<if_matched_sid>5715</if_matched_sid>`. Without that, Wazuh may only fire the base rule (5715) and never your custom rule, especially if your custom rule has no additional match conditions.

Use this (note the `dstuser` field shown in your logtest output):

```xml name=local_rules.xml
<rule id="110002" level="10">
  <if_matched_sid>5715</if_matched_sid>
  <regex field="dstuser">^azureuser$</regex>
  <description>Successful SSH login as azureuser.</description>
  <mitre><id>T1021</id></mitre>
</rule>
```

Then:

```bash
sudo /var/ossec/bin/wazuh-analysisd -t
sudo systemctl restart wazuh-manager
sudo /var/ossec/bin/wazuh-logtest
```

In `wazuh-logtest`, you should see **rule 5715** fired first, then **rule 110002**.

If `regex field="dstuser"` gives an error on your version, use a simple text match instead:

```xml name=local_rules.xml
<rule id="110002" level="10">
  <if_matched_sid>5715</if_matched_sid>
  <match>Accepted</match>
  <match>for azureuser</match>
  <description>Successful SSH login as azureuser.</description>
  <mitre><id>T1021</id></mitre>
</rule>
```

If you paste the next `wazuh-logtest` output after this change, I’ll confirm it’s matching correctly.

Di config kamu juga ada **ID rule duplikat** (`100001` dipakai dua kali) → ini bisa bikin rule tidak dimuat benar.

Your syscheck config looks fine for **/etc** (realtime enabled), so a comment edit **should** trigger a FIM alert. If it didn’t, the most likely causes are:

---

## ✅ 1) You edited the file, but the *agent* doesn’t have FIM enabled (or isn’t sending)
This config is on the **agent**, so confirm the agent is running and sending FIM:

```bash
sudo systemctl status wazuh-agent --no-pager
sudo tail -n 200 /var/ossec/logs/ossec.log | egrep -i "syscheck|fim|realtime|error"
```

---

## ✅ 2) Realtime FIM doesn’t trigger on some editors
Certain editors update files by **atomic replace** (create temp + rename). That *should* still trigger, but sometimes the underlying FS + inotify doesn’t fire as expected.

**Test using direct append:**
```bash
echo "# SOC Rule Test Validation" | sudo tee -a /etc/passwd
```

Then immediately check agent log:
```bash
sudo tail -n 100 /var/ossec/logs/ossec.log | egrep -i "syscheck|fim|checksum|passwd"
```

---

## ✅ 3) The change triggers, but **alert** isn’t created
FIM generates **syscheck events** that may not meet alert threshold or rules.

Check raw alert/events:

```bash
# On the manager
sudo tail -n 200 /var/ossec/logs/alerts/alerts.json | jq '.rule.id,.rule.description,.full_log'
```

Also check the **archives** (contains raw events even if alert not fired):

```bash
sudo tail -n 200 /var/ossec/logs/archives/archives.json | jq '.decoder.name,.full_log'
```

If you see `syscheck` entries in **archives.json** but not **alerts.json**, then it’s just a rules/level threshold issue.

---

## ✅ 4) Confirm agent-side FIM is actually running
Run:
```bash
sudo /var/ossec/bin/syscheck_control -s
```

And force a scan:
```bash
sudo /var/ossec/bin/syscheck_control -r
```

i think it works just fine, when i tried to create `touch /etc/temp` it shows the file creation alert same as when i delete it. then i edit resolve.conf by adding a comment it didn't trigger the alert, then i edit my hostname file and it trigger the file modified alert

It’s expected on Ubuntu: **`/etc/resolv.conf` is usually a symlink**, so when you edit it, the real file being changed is **outside `/etc`** (often under `/run/systemd/resolve/`). Since your FIM only watches `/etc`, that change won’t trigger.

That’s why:
- `hostname` (real file in `/etc`) triggered ✅  
- `/etc/resolv.conf` (symlink → target elsewhere) did **not** ❌

### Check where it points:

ls -l /etc/resolv.conf
readlink -f /etc/resolv.conf
```

If you see something like:
```
/run/systemd/resolve/stub-resolv.conf
```

Add this to your `<syscheck>`:

```xml

```

Then restart agent:

```

Kalau **/etc/hostname** memicu alert tapi **/etc/passwd** tidak, biasanya bukan karena FIM‑nya mati, tapi karena salah satu dari hal ini:

## 1) **/etc/passwd di-*ignore* oleh konfigurasi group/agent.conf**
Di Wazuh, **config agent bisa di-override** dari manager lewat `agent.conf` (group config).  
Bisa saja di group config ada:
```xml
<ignore>/etc/passwd</ignore>
```

**Cek di manager:**
```bash
sudo grep -R "passwd" -n /var/ossec/etc/shared /var/ossec/etc/agent.conf
```

---

## 2) Alert masuk ke **archives.json**, tapi tidak naik ke **alerts.json**
Syscheck event bisa ada, tapi rule level rendah sehingga tidak “alert”.

**Cek di manager:**
```bash
sudo tail -n 200 /var/ossec/logs/archives/archives.json | grep -i "/etc/passwd"
```

Kalau ada di archives tapi tidak di alerts → ini masalah **rule/level**, bukan FIM.

---

## 3) Perubahan cepat lalu “kembali normal”
Jika kamu edit lalu file segera “dibalikkan” (misalnya oleh tool/automation), scan realtime bisa melihat kondisi akhir sama dengan sebelum, jadi tidak ada alert.

---

## 4) Kamu mengubah file di **manager**, bukan di **agent**
Pastikan kamu edit `/etc/passwd` di agent yang benar (go-health-api), bukan di server manager.

---

# Cara tes paling pasti
Di **agent**:
```bash
sudo /var/ossec/bin/syscheck_control -r
sudo bash -c 'echo "FIM_TEST" >> /etc/passwd'
```

Di **manager**:
```bash
sudo tail -n 200 /var/ossec/logs/alerts/alerts.json | grep -i "/etc/passwd"
sudo tail -n 200 /var/ossec/logs/archives/archives.json | grep -i "/etc/passwd"
```

---

Kalau kamu kirim hasil:
- `grep -R "passwd" -n /var/ossec/etc/shared /var/ossec/etc/agent.conf`
- potongan `archives.json` setelah perubahan

how do i test rule id 110007

Yang kamu maksud **rule 110007** yang mana?  
Di percakapan sebelumnya, 110007 adalah **SSH brute‑force** (frequency/timeframe + same_source_ip). Kalau iya, cara test‑nya:

---

## ✅ Test rule 110007 (brute force)
Rule ini butuh **jumlah event tertentu** dari **IP yang sama** dalam waktu singkat. Misalnya:

```xml
<rule id="100007" level="10" frequency="5" timeframe="60">
  <if_matched_sid>5760, 5710</if_matched_sid>
  <same_source_ip />
  <description>ALERT: High frequency SSH brute force attack detected from the same Source IP.</description>
</rule>
```

### Metode 1 — **wazuh-logtest** (paling aman)
```bash
sudo /var/ossec/bin/wazuh-logtest
```

Paste **5 baris** log failed SSH dari IP yang sama dalam waktu cepat:

```
Apr 07 18:10:01 host sshd[1001]: Failed password for invalid user root from 8.8.8.8 port 50001 ssh2
Apr 07 18:10:02 host sshd[1002]: Failed password for invalid user root from 8.8.8.8 port 50002 ssh2
Apr 07 18:10:03 host sshd[1003]: Failed password for invalid user root from 8.8.8.8 port 50003 ssh2
Apr 07 18:10:04 host sshd[1004]: Failed password for invalid user root from 8.8.8.8 port 50004 ssh2
Apr 07 18:10:05 host sshd[1005]: Failed password for invalid user root from 8.8.8.8 port 50005 ssh2
```

Pada baris ke‑5, **rule 110007** harus “fire”.

---

### Metode 2 — **real SSH attempts**
Dari host lain, lakukan 5 login gagal dalam <60 detik ke SSH server kamu.

---

## Jika tidak “fired”
Paste output `wazuh-logtest` setelah baris ke‑5, dan aku cek apakah:
- base rule (`5710` / `5760`) memang match
- `srcip` terbaca dengan benar
- `same_source_ip` sudah bekerja

Untuk memastikan **rule 110007** benar‑benar match, ada 2 hal penting:

1) **Rule 110007‑mu harus “mendapat” event dasar** (if_matched_sid)  
2) Log yang kamu kirim lewat `logger` harus **ter‑decode sebagai sshd** (program_name = sshd) dan menghasilkan **srcip**

---

# ✅ Cara test yang paling akurat

## 1) Pastikan definisi rule 110007
Paste rule 110007 kamu yang **terakhir** (sekarang).  
Aku butuh memastikan `if_matched_sid`‑nya benar.

---

## 2) Kirim log pakai logger **dengan tag sshd**
```
logger -t sshd "Failed password for invalid user root from 8.8.8.8 port 50001 ssh2"
```

Lakukan **5 kali** dengan port berbeda dalam <60 detik.

> **Kenapa tag penting?**  
> Kalau tag bukan `sshd`, decoder tidak akan match dan `srcip` tidak terbentuk → rule brute force tidak jalan.

---

## 3) Cek dengan `wazuh-logtest` dulu (paling jelas)
Ini akan tunjukkan `decoder`, `srcip`, dan rule yang match:

```bash
sudo /var/ossec/bin/wazuh-logtest
```

Paste 5 baris logmu.  
Kalau `Phase 2` tidak muncul `srcip`, brute‑force rule tidak bisa jalan.

<group name="local,custom,linux,journald,">

  <!-- 1) SSH brute force (agregasi kegagalan login)
       Catatan: SID yang dipakai di bawah mengacu ke rule "SSH authentication failed".
       Kalau di environment-mu SID-nya beda, kita tinggal sesuaikan dari event yang muncul. -->
  <rule id="110001" level="12">
    <if_matched_sid>5716</if_matched_sid>
    <frequency>10</frequency>
    <timeframe>120</timeframe>
    <description>SSH brute force suspected: 10+ failed SSH logins in 2 minutes.</description>
    <mitre><id>T1110</id></mitre>
  </rule>

  <!-- 2) SSH login sukses sebagai root -->
  <rule id="110002" level="10">
    <if_matched_sid>5715</if_matched_sid>
    <field name="user">root</field>
    <description>Successful SSH login as root.</description>
    <mitre><id>T1021</id></mitre>
  </rule>

  <!-- 3) Privilege escalation: penggunaan sudo (jurnald/syslog)
       Ini match teks umum "sudo:" yang biasanya muncul di auth logs/journald -->
  <rule id="110003" level="7">
    <if_group>journald,syslog</if_group>
    <match>sudo:</match>
    <description>Privilege escalation activity: sudo command executed (check user and command).</description>
    <mitre><id>T1548</id></mitre>
  </rule>

  <!-- 4) Privilege escalation: penggunaan su untuk ganti user (mis. ke root) -->
  <rule id="110004" level="9">
    <if_group>journald,syslog</if_group>
    <match>session opened for user root</match>
    <description>Privilege escalation activity: session opened for user root (possible su/sudo).</description>
    <mitre><id>T1548</id></mitre>
  </rule>

  <!-- 5) Indikasi malware/persistence via install paket mencurigakan (dpkg.log)
       Ini bukan "pasti malware", tapi useful untuk alert saat ada install tool yang sering dipakai attacker.
       Tambah/kurangi daftar sesuai kebutuhan. -->
  <rule id="110005" level="10">
    <if_group>syslog</if_group>
    <match>/var/log/dpkg.log</match>
    <regex>install\s+(curl|wget|nmap|netcat|nc|socat|tor|proxychains|john|hydra|masscan|tcpdump|wireshark|tshark)\b</regex>
    <description>Suspicious package installation detected in dpkg.log (potential attacker tooling).</description>
    <mitre><id>T1105</id></mitre>
  </rule>

</group>

<group name="local,custom,ssh,ubuntu,">

  <!-- A) SSH login sukses untuk user tertentu (azureuser) -->
  <rule id="110002" level="10">
    <if_group>syslog,journald</if_group>
    <regex>sshd\[[0-9]+\]: Accepted (password|publickey|keyboard-interactive) for azureuser from ([0-9]{1,3}\.){3}[0-9]{1,3}</regex>
    <description>Successful SSH login as azureuser.</description>
    <mitre><id>T1021</id></mitre>
  </rule>

  <!-- B) SSH login sukses sebagai root (lebih high severity) -->
  <rule id="110006" level="12">
    <if_group>syslog,journald</if_group>
    <regex>sshd\[[0-9]+\]: Accepted (password|publickey|keyboard-interactive) for root from ([0-9]{1,3}\.){3}[0-9]{1,3}</regex>
    <description>Successful SSH login as root.</description>
    <mitre><id>T1021</id></mitre>
  </rule>

  <!-- C) Brute force: banyak "Failed password" ke sshd (agregasi sederhana)
       Ini akan memicu kalau ada 10 kegagalan dalam 2 menit (campur semua IP).
       Kalau kamu mau per-IP, kita perlu lihat field srcip yang diekstrak Wazuh di alertmu. -->
  <rule id="110001" level="12">
    <if_group>syslog,journald</if_group>
    <regex>sshd\[[0-9]+\]: Failed password for (invalid user )?.+ from ([0-9]{1,3}\.){3}[0-9]{1,3}</regex>
    <frequency>10</frequency>
    <timeframe>120</timeframe>
    <description>SSH brute force suspected: 10+ failed password attempts in 2 minutes.</description>
    <mitre><id>T1110</id></mitre>
  </rule>

</group>
